coredata.dc:title: Chiral dual-emission composite material fluorescein/CCQDs @ZIF-8 for highly efficient recognition of phenylenediamine isomers and their oxidized product

coredata.dc:description: As a new type of fluorescent nanomaterial, chiral carbon quantum dots (CCQDs) have the advantages of wide source, good water solubility and high chemical stability, and have been widely used in drug detection, bioimaging and chemical sensing. In this work, a chiral dual-emission hybrid material fluorescein/CCQDs@ZIF-8 (1) was synthesized by in-situ encapsulation strategy. Luminescence emission position of CCQDs and fluorescein are almost unchanged after the encapsulation into ZIF-8. The luminescent emissions of CCQDs and fluorescein can be observed to be located at 430 nm and 513 nm, respectively. When 1 is soaked in pure water, ethanol, dimethylsulfoxide, DMF, DMA and targeted substances solution for 24 h, 1 can maintain its structural stability. Photo-luminescent (PL) studies show that 1 can discriminate p-phenylenediamine (PPD) from m-phenylenediamine (MPD) and o-phenylenediamine (OPD), which can detect the presence of PPD with high sensitivity and selectivity (ratiomeric fluorescent probe with KBH: 1.85 × 103 M−1 and detection limit: 8.51 μM). Further, 1 also effectively distinguish the oxidized product of these phenylenediamine(PD) isomers. 1 can be used as a “turn-off” fluorescent probe to detect oxidized product of PPD (ratiomeric fluorescent probe with KSV: 6.82 × 102 M−1 and detection limit: 0.112 mM) and a “turn-on” fluorescent probe to detect oxidized product of MPD (ratiomeric fluorescent probe: KBH: 1.65 × 103 M−1 and detection limit: 35.03 μM) and oxidized product of OPD (ratiomeric fluorescent probe: KBH: 2.40 × 106 M−1 and detection limit: 0.105 μM). Further, for the convenience of practical application, 1 can be developed as fluorescence ink and be prepared into a mixed matrix membrane. When the target substances are gradually added to the membrane, significant luminescence change with obvious color change can be observed.

head.ce:title: Chiral dual-emission composite material fluorescein/CCQDs @ZIF-8 for highly efficient recognition of phenylenediamine isomers and their oxidized product

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a new type of fluorescent nanomaterial, chiral carbon quantum dots (CCQDs) have the advantages of wide source, good water solubility and high chemical stability, and have been widely used in drug detection, bioimaging and chemical sensing. In this work, a chiral dual-emission hybrid material fluorescein/CCQDs@ZIF-8 (1) was synthesized by in-situ encapsulation strategy. Luminescence emission position of CCQDs and fluorescein are almost unchanged after the encapsulation into ZIF-8. The luminescent emissions of CCQDs and fluorescein can be observed to be located at 430 nm and 513 nm, respectively. When 1 is soaked in pure water, ethanol, dimethylsulfoxide, DMF, DMA and targeted substances solution for 24 h, 1 can maintain its structural stability. Photo-luminescent (PL) studies show that 1 can discriminate p-phenylenediamine (PPD) from m-phenylenediamine (MPD) and o-phenylenediamine (OPD), which can detect the presence of PPD with high sensitivity and selectivity (ratiomeric fluorescent probe with KBH: 1.85 × 103 M−1 and detection limit: 8.51 μM). Further, 1 also effectively distinguish the oxidized product of these phenylenediamine(PD) isomers. 1 can be used as a “turn-off” fluorescent probe to detect oxidized product of PPD (ratiomeric fluorescent probe with KSV: 6.82 × 102 M−1 and detection limit: 0.112 mM) and a “turn-on” fluorescent probe to detect oxidized product of MPD (ratiomeric fluorescent probe: KBH: 1.65 × 103 M−1 and detection limit: 35.03 μM) and oxidized product of OPD (ratiomeric fluorescent probe: KBH: 2.40 × 106 M−1 and detection limit: 0.105 μM). Further, for the convenience of practical application, 1 can be developed as fluorescence ink and be prepared into a mixed matrix membrane. When the target substances are gradually added to the membrane, significant luminescence change with obvious color change can be observed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have been regarded as a new kind of multifunctional porous composite material, which has high adjustability based on the combination of inorganic and organic structures [1]. Due to the regular order arrange, the resulting MOFs-based materials have numerous excellent properties, such as adjustable pore structure, high loading ability and high porosity [2]. These materials have the broad applications in catalysis [3], gas adsorption and separation [4,5], drug delivery [6] and chemical sensing. For example, Shi et al use a bifunctional molecular material to realize the highly efficient recognition and removal of persistent organic pollutants [7]. Zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIFs) are a class of three-dimensional MOFs connected by metal ions and imidazole organic ligands [8-10], ZIF-8 can be used as the ideal encapsulating material to load carbon dots or other nanoparticles.

In the recent years, quantum dots (QDs) has received more attention because of its outstanding optical properties [11]. Among them, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted more attention due to its low toxicity and high fluorescence stability [12]. CDs are mainly composed of natural biology (e.g., grape skins, fruit juice and aloe) [13-15] and organic compounds (e.g., citric acid, L-tryptophan and phenylenediamine) [16-18]. Chiral carbon quantum dots (CCQDs) are a kind of special CDs, which was first proposed in 2016 by Suzuki et al. [19]. L-CDs and D-CDs have different recognize ability to different chiral substances. In terms of chemistry, CCQDs can mobilize the property of chemical substances. Researchers have discovered the application of CCQDs in biology and enantioselective recognition of chiral substances, but less attention has been paid to highly efficiently detect chemical isomers by using CCQDs. On the other hand, fluorescein is an aromatic dye with hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, which has the high fluorescent substance. Fluorescein was firstly discovered by Von Bayer in 1871 as a xanthene dye [20]. As a kind of multifunctional dyes, fluorescein and its derivatives have excellent fluorescence properties and physical properties [21,22]. Fluorescein and its derivatives can be used as fluorescent markers in many studies, but their research on chemical sensing still needs to be further studied.

Phenylenediamine (PD) is a dye intermediate, which has been widely used in hair and leather production, it has three isomers, p-, m- and o-phenylenediamine. The most common application of p-phenylenediamine (PPD) is used as the main intermediate of hair dye [23]. Researchers have found that PPD can be oxidized to form Bandrowskis base (BB) [24,25]. The possible products of PPD oxidation are 4-nitroaniline and 4,4′-azodianiline [26], p-benzoquinone (PBQ) [27] or quinonediimine (in the presence of coupling agent like H2O2) [23]. And the oxidation products may be affected by pH. Under alkaline conditions, main oxidation products of PPD are the red–purple trimeric compounds Bandrowskis base (BB) and quinone. With the increase of acidity of solution, the content of BB in oxidation products increased [27]. Similarly, m-phenylenediamine (MPD) can be widely used in chemical industry and has been found to be carcinogenic [28,29]. But the properties of MPD are relatively stable and the degree of oxidation is weak in air compared with PPD. Another isomer, o-phenylenediamine (OPD), can be used in the production of pesticides and drugs, and is an important intermediate in the production process [30]. The oxidation products of OPD may be 2, 2′-diaminoazobenzene or 2, 3-diaminophenazine [31,32]. Researchers have conducted preliminary studies on the fluorescence properties of PD isomers and their oxidation products [33,34], but there are few quantitative and qualitative studies on the oxidation degree of these PD isomers [35].

In this work, the composite material fluorescein/CCQDs@ZIF-8 (1) was synthesized by “one-pot” method to realize highly selective and sensitive detection of phenylenediamine (PD) isomers and their oxidation products (Scheme 1 ). Both chiral carbon quantum dots (CCQDs) and fluorescein can be encapsulated into ZIF-8 through in-situ encapsulation strategy. Multiple fluorescence bands located at 430 nm and 513 nm can be observed, which can be ascribed to CCQDs and fluorescein emissions in 1, respectively. Based on PXRD, TEM and photo-luminescent results, it can be confirmed that composite dual-emission material Fluorescein/CCQDs@ZIF-8 (1) is successfully prepared. Photoluminescence (PL) experiments demonstrate that 1 can be used as ratiomeric fluorescent probe for newly prepared PPD. Moreover, 1 can be used as “turn-off” fluorescent probe to detect oxidized product of PPD and “turn-on” fluorescent probe to detect oxidized product of MPD and oxidized product of OPD. 1 can be developed as fluorescence ink and be prepared into a mixed matrix membrane. It is interesting that when the target substances are gradually added to the membrane, significant luminescence change with obvious color change can be observed, which also make that 1 has more piratical application prospects.

Perkin-Elmer 240 factor analyzer was developed to analyze C, H and N elements. Shimadzu UV-2600 ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer developed to analyze the digital spectrum. FT-IR spectrum (4000–400 cm−1) was recorded by utilizing NICOLET 6700 FT-IR spectrometer (NICOLET, USA). The mixed powders with 1:150 ratio of 1 and spectral pure potassium bromide were formed into transparent films for infrared spectrum testing. Aqueous solution of 1 with the concentration of 0.2 g/L was filtered through a 450 nm filter membrane and dropped onto the copper mesh for TEM test. Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) sample is used to be characterized using a Rigaku D/Max-2500 X-ray diffractometer geared up with Cu-K rays. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) can be used to investigate the morphology of 1, ZIF-8 and CCQDs, which have been captured the usage of Tecnai G2 F20 (FEI, USA). It operates on an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. Fluorescence characterization has been recorded on a Shimadzu RF-5301 spectrophotometer.

Synthetic method of CCQDs was reported in previous work [16]. In this work, “one-pot” method was adopted. 10 mL ethanol solution containing 252.6 mg Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, 2 mL CCQDs and 0.0019 g fluorescein was stirred for 15 min. Subsequently, a uniform distribution solution of 2-methylimidazole (2-MIM, 410.7 mg) was gradually added into the mixture. The mixed solution was stirred for 15 h. The resulting suspension was centrifuged at 6000 r for 10 min and washed three times with ethanol to remove the impurities. The light yellow solid was filtered and dried at room temperature. FT-IR data (cm−1, KBr): 3419(w), 3132(w), 2927(w), 1571(m), 1455(m), 1420(s), 1307(m), 1175(w), 1143(s), 994(m), 755(m), 691(w). Yield: 91.11 % for 1 based on ZIF-8.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photo-luminescent (PL) experiment for highly detection of newly prepared PD isomers and their oxidized products based on 1
ce:section.ce:para: The sample of 0.0001 g 1 is dissolved into 1 mL purified water to prepare the 0.1 mg/mL concentration for detecting photoluminescence (PL) experiment. Targeted PD substances were dissolved in DMSO(dimethylsulfoxide) or ethanol to form the solution with 0.01 mmol/mL concentrations, respectively. The photoluminescence detection of the newly prepared phenylenediamine isomers were performed on the day of solution preparation, and the luminescence effect of oxidized phenylenediamine isomers on the product were studied by using the same solution in the following 7 days. In the titration process, analytical solution is injected into solution of 1 at a dose of 10 μL per drop, PL experiment is tested under 345 nm excitation wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of mixed matrix membrane based on 1
ce:section.ce:para: Mixed matrix membrane based on fluorescein/CCQDs@ZIF-8(1) was prepared for practical application. Agar (0.0850 g) and powder of 1 (0.0024 g) were well mixed, 10 mL pure water were then added, the mixture was heated and stirred at 100 ℃, the agar is completely dissolved to obtain a uniform suspension solution. The suspension is then poured into the mold and naturally is cooled to room temperature, which ultimately can obtain the mixed matrix membrane. When the target material is dropped to mixed matrix membrane of 1, luminescence variation with obvious color changes can be observed under the ultraviolet light of 365 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PXRD, FT-IR and TEM characterizations of fluorescein/CCQDs@ZIF-8(1)
ce:section.ce:para: PXRD pattern of 1 is coincident with simulated data of ZIF-8 [36–38]. The structure of 1 has good solvent stability, even if it is immersed in ethanol solution, water, DMF and DMA for 24 h, ZIF-8 skeleton of 1 can maintain unchanged (Fig. 1 ). The result illustrates 1 has excellent solvent stability.

FT-IR spectra of 1 were studied from 4000 to 400 cm−1 (Fig. S1). The peak located at 3419 cm−1 shows the telescopic vibration of N–H, and the peak of 3131 cm−1 shows the telescopic vibration of C–H bond on the imidazole ring [39-42]. The peak located at 2927 cm−1 is ascribed to telescopic vibration of methyl C–H on 2-methylimidazolium. The peak located at 1571 cm−1 shows C=C telescopic vibration of imidazole ring. These peaks of 1455 cm−1, 1420 cm−1, 1306 cm−1, 1175 cm−1 and 994 cm−1 show the presence of an imidazole ring [43–46]. Peaks located at 1579 cm−1, 1568 cm−1 and 753 cm−1 indicate benzene ring of CCQDs. Because of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on fluorescein, these functional groups form hydrogen bonds with nitrogen atom in ZIF-8, which increases the intensity of the peak located at 3419 cm−1 and widens the vibration band spectrum. The conjugate system in fluorescein and π-π stacking effect on ZIF-8 make that three wavelength numbers 1571 cm−1, 1456 cm−1 and 1420 cm−1 move to lower wave numbers.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to characterized the morphologies of 1. As shown in Fig. 2 a, CCQDs have an average particle size of 4.3 nm [47–50] and can be uniformly distributed in aqueous solution. 1 has a hexagonal ZIF-8 structure with multiple channels. The skeleton surface of 1 is irregular and connected with lamellar MOFs structure (Fig. 2b). By irradiation of the lamellar structures surrounding the MOF skeleton, it was clear that a large amount of CCQDs and fluorescein was encapsulated into ZIF-8 (Fig. 2c) [51-53]. Further, luminescence characteristic bands also reveal the typical luminescent emissions of CCQDs and fluorescein [54,55]. As shown in Fig. S2, in the synthetic composite material 1, CCQDs-based emission occurs at 444 nm and fluorescein-based emission happens at 515 nm under the 350 nm excitation wavelength. Emission spectra of 1 also show that both CCQDs and fluorescein have been successfully encapsulated in ZIF-8 skeleton forming fluorescein/CCQDs@ZIF-8(1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Highly efficient detection of PD isomers based on 1
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate luminescence property of 1 and the ability to recognize PD isomers, aqueous solution of 1 with the concentration of 0.1 mg/mL is prepared. 3 mL aqueous solution was transferred into a quartz cuvette, and then 0.01 mmol/mL PD ethanol solutions were gradually added for fluorescence titration under 345 nm excitation wavelength. In this work, luminescence recognition difference between newly prepared ethanol solution of o-phenylenediamine (OPD), m-phenylenediamine (MPD) and p-phenylenediamine (PPD) were studied. The result shows that PPD has the most obvious fluorescent enhancement phenomenon. For 1, it may be considered as an outstanding single CCQDs-based emission (430 nm) fluorescence probe for detection of PPD (Fig. 3 a). After PPD was added, fluorescence emission intensity at 430 nm in 1 is obviously enhanced compared with adding OPD and MPD, but the fluorescence intensity located at 513 nm has no obvious change (Fig. 3b and S3a).

It can be observed that, when 10 μL PPD each time was added, fluorescence intensity of 1 uniformly increased (Fig. 4 a). Benesi-Hildebrand (BH) curve is plotted to describe the linear relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio (I/I0) value of 1 and the concentration of PPD (Fig. S3b). In the range of 0–0.6 mM, the value of I/I0 increases with the increase of concentration. The curve I/I0 = KBH[A] + C was used to calculate the value of KBH, I and I0 stand the fluorescence intensity of 1 with and without PPD, respectively. [A] means the added concentration of PPD and C is a constant. KBH is the enhancement coefficient and calculated as 1.68 × 103 M−1. LOD is 17.49 μM (LOD = 3σ/k, Where σ is the standard deviation of blank measurement, and k is the fluorescence enhancement constant) [56–58]. It can be seen that 1 can detect PPD with high sensitivity and discernibility. Fluorescein-based emission is almost constant, and CCQDs-based emission in 1 increases regularly with the increase of the concentration of PPD. In addition to use the fluorescence change of CCQDs-based emission in 1 to measure PPD, the ratiomeric value of I430/I513 also can be used as the ordinate and the concentration of PPD was used as the abscissa for linear fitting (Fig. 4b). The curve I/I0 = KBH[A] + C also can be used in ratio fluorescent probe. Calculated curve is I430/I513 = 0.00185[μM] + 1.09. R2 of this ratio probe is 0.998, the enhancement efficiency KBH is 1.85 × 103 M−1, LOD is 8.51 μM (LOD = 3σ/k, Where σ is the standard deviation of blank measurement, and k is the fluorescence enhancement constant) [59,60]. Therefore 1 can be used either as a single CCQDs-based emission fluorescent probe or a ratiometric fluorescent probe for highly detection of newly prepared PPD. It has the advantages of high sensitivity, low detection limit and high resolution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Highly efficient detection of oxidized PPD substances based on 1
ce:section.ce:para: Further, the performance of 1 in detecting the oxidized PD isomers for 7 days was studied. In this study, photoluminescence characteristics and pH changes of PD oxidation products are continuously monitored for 7 days. Similarly, aqueous solution of 1 with the concentration of 0.1 mg/mL was prepared. 3 mL aqueous water solution was transferred into a quartz cuvette, and then 0.01 mmol/mL oxidized PD ethanol solution was added drop by drop for fluorescence titration. The result shows that the oxidized PPD ethanol solution can reduce the fluorescence intensity of 1, especially fluorescein-based emission is obviously reduced (Fig. 5 a and 5b). And CCQDs-based emission in 1 is blue-shifted in the presence of oxidized PPD. At the same time, when oxidized MPD ethanol solution is added, CCQDs-based and fluorescein-based emissions in 1 can be efficiently enhanced (Fig. 5c). Unlike PPD and MPD, when oxidized OPD ethanol solution is added, fluorescein-based emission in 1 increases and is also red-shifted from 513 nm to 550 nm (Fig. 5d). Oxidized PPD, MPD and OPD can be efficiently distinguished by fluorescence intensities change of 1.

Fluorescence titration of oxidized PD isomers in the ethanol solution was analyzed. As shown in Fig. 6 , CCQDs-based and fluorescein-based emissions decrease with the addition of oxidized PPD, when oxidized PPD was added, fluorescein-based emission significantly decreased (Fig. S4a). In contrast, while oxidized MPD and OPD can enhance CCQDs-based and fluorescein-based emissions in 1, oxidized MPD and OPD can cause fluorescence enhancement of 1 (Fig. S4b and S4c). Oxidized MPD mainly interacts with CCQDs-based emission in 1 to produce fluorescence response, while oxidized OPD mainly enhance the fluorescein-based emission of 1.

Stern-Volmer (SV) curve is plotted to describe the linear relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio (I0/I) value of 1 and the concentration of oxidized PPD (Fig. 7 a and 7b). The concentration of oxidized PPD ranges from 0 to 0.4 mM, I0/I = KSV[A] + C is used to calculate the value of KSV, I and I0 stand the fluorescence intensity of 1 with and without oxidized PPD, respectively. C is a constant and [A] stands the concentration of oxidized PPD adding in 1. The fitted curve equation of fluorescein-based emission fluorescence intensity is I0/I = 0.00116[μM] + 1.02 with the R2 of 0.994. KSV is the quenching efficiency and calculated as 1.16 × 103 M−1. LOD is calculated as 81.13 μM (LOD = 3σ/k, Where σ is the standard deviation of blank measurement, and k is the fluorescence enhancement constant).

Further, 1 can not only be used as a fluorescent probe based on fluorescein-based emission for oxidized PPD detection, but also use the CCQDs-based and fluorescein-based emissions as ratiomeric fluorescent sensor. Herein, fluorescence intensities of I430/I513 and oxidized PPD concentration were plotted. Fitting result can also be described by Stern-Volmer (SV) curve (Fig. 7c). Fitted curve equation is I430/I513 = 0.000682[μM] + 0.975, which R2 is 0.996. LOD is calculated as 0.112 mM (LOD = 3σ/k, Where σ is the standard deviation of blank measurement, and k is the fluorescence enhancement constant), KBH is 6.82 × 102 M−1. The value of I430/I513 varies linearly with concentration of oxidized PPD. This also allows 1 to be used as ratiomeric fluorescent probe for the detection of oxidized PPD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Highly efficient detection of oxidized MPD target substances based on 1
ce:section.ce:para: In contrast, ethanol solution of oxidized MPD can enhance the fluorescence intensity of 1. Benesi-Hildebrand (BH) curve I/I0 = KBH[A] + C was used to analyze the learn relationship between the ratio of fluorescence intensity (I/I0) and the concentration of oxidized MPD (Fig. 8 a). I0 and I represent fluorescence intensity with and without oxidized MPD ethanol solution, respectively. C is a constant and [A] stands the concentration of oxidized MPD adding in 1. For the CCQDs-based emission in 1, fitted curve equation was I/I0 = 0.00291[μM] + 0.939 with R2 of 0.997 (Fig. 8b). KBH was enhancement efficiency and calculated as 2.91 × 103 M−1, LOD is 56.64 μM and R2 is 0.997 indicate that 1 is a high sensitivity fluorescence probe for oxidized MPD (LOD = 3σ/k, Where σ is the standard deviation of blank measurement, and k is the fluorescence enhancement constant). For fluorescein-based emission in 1, fitted curve equation was I/I0 = 0.000804[μM] + 0.975 with R2 of 0.997 (Fig. 8c) [61]. Enhancement efficiency KBH was calculated as 8.04 × 102 M−1, LOD is 71.88 μM. Further, 1 can also be used as a dual-emission fluorescent probe for efficient detection of oxidized MPD with high sensitivity and accuracy. In addition, both CCQDs-based and fluorescein-based emission are enhanced at different amplitudes after oxidized MPD is added, the relationship between the ratio change of two fluorescence emissions and oxidized MPD concentration can be observed. The resulting linear equation is I430/I513 = 0.00165[μM] + 1.03 with R2 is 0.998. K is 1.65 × 103 M−1 and LOD is 0.135 mM (LOD = 3σ/k, Where σ is the standard deviation of blank measurement, and k is the fluorescence enhancement constant) [61]. The fitting curve clearly shows that the ratio of fluorescence intensities is positively correlated with oxidized MPD concentration (Fig. 8d). Therefore, 1 can also be used as a highly efficient ratiomeric fluorescent probe to quantitatively analyze the concentration of oxidized MPD in ethanol solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Highly efficient detection of oxidized OPD target substances based on 1
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 9 a, 1 can also highly efficiently detect oxidized OPD in ethanol solution. When oxidized OPD was gradually added, fluorescein-based emission in 1 significantly increased, while CCQDs-based emission also slightly increases. With the increase of oxidized OPD concentration, luminescence position of fluorescein-based emission in 1 is gradually red-shifted to 550 nm. The ratio between fluorescein-based emission (I/I0) and concentration of 1 was linearly analyzed by Benesi-Hildebrand (BH) curve. As shown in Fig. 9b and 9c, fitting equation is I/I0 = 0.0193[μM] + 0.879 with R2 of 0.999. KBH means fluorescence enhancement efficiency and is calculated to be 1.93 × 104 M−1, LOD is 13.04 μM. Similarly, Benesi-Hildebrand (BH) curves can be used to linearly fit the relationship between CCQDs-based emission and oxidized OPD concentration (Fig. 9d). The resulting curve is I/I0 = 0.000598[μM] + 0.970 with R2 of 0.992. Enhancement efficiency is 5.98 × 102 M−1 and LOD is 0.42 mM. Meanwhile, according to intensities changes of CCQDs-based and fluorescein-based emissions, 1 can be used as a ratiomeric fluorescent probe to detect oxidized OPD. Nonlinear fitting result is I430/I550 = 2.40*exp(-C/73.7) + 0.455, C is the analyst concentration, R2 is 0.99. Fluorescence enhancement efficiency K is 2.40 × 106 M−1, LOD is calculated as 0.105 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection mechanism on the detection of oxidized PD target substances
ce:section.ce:para: Compared with newly formulated PD, oxidized PD isomers have better discrimination and solvent selectivity. UV–vis fluorescence spectra were investigated (Fig. S7), absorption peak located at 240 nm can be attributed to π-π* transition of C–C bond in aromatic molecules [62]. After adding the target substance to 1, the absorbance located at 240 nm increased, indicating that there exist complexation between the oxidized PD target substance and 1. In addition, after the target substance in ethanol solution was added to 1, absorbance of target substance increased at about 260 nm, which proved the existence of hydrogen bond interactions. In particular, there were two new absorption peaks after the addition of OPD, one was less than 240 nm and another was higher than 240 nm, which was the same as the above fluorescence titration result. In addition, the stability of 1 was also studied, the aqueous solution of 1 were soaked in target substances for 24 h and PXRD pattern result indicated that 1 can maintain its structure after soaking in these PD isomers (Fig. S8). PD isomers should change the fluorescence of 1 by the complexation between 1 and these PD isomers rather than through the structure destruction of 1.

The oxidized OPD dissolved in ethanol also can make fluorescein-based emission is enhanced and red-shift, while the intensity of CCQDs-based emission almost has no change. This may be ascribed that amine groups in the oxidized OPD are closer, which is conducive to intramolecular hydrogen bonds and the formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonds between oxidized OPD, ethanol and fluorescein molecules. Xu et al. reported that the enhancement of the π-conjugate system by π-π superposition resulted in red-shifted in CDs fluorescence [63]. Illustrating that oxidized OPD in ethanol can enhance the π-conjugate system of fluorescein. The result also illustrate that hydrogen bonds exist widely between PD isomers and ethanol in the detection process, for example, the Cu-MOF Material can be constructed with a triazine polycarboxylate skeleton, which has multifunctional identify and microdetecting of the aromatic diamine family (o,m,p-Phenylenediamine) based on the luminescent response [64].

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH variation and time effect on the detection of oxidized PD target substances based on 1
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. S5, when the suspended time of these PD isomers were placed in the atmosphere, the color of PD isomer solution is gradually changing, indicating that the oxidized degree of these PD isomers increases. And the solution obtained by PPD and dissolved in ethanol were weakly alkaline with pH was 7.71 and 7.13, respectively, while OPD dissolved in ethanol was neutral with pH is 6.94 (Fig. S6). After 7 days of oxidation, the pH of PPD solution was almost unchanged with pH was 7.86, the alkalinity of OPD solution was slightly enhanced to 7.29, and MPD solution became weakly acidic with pH was 6.6.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application as fluorescence ink based on fluorescein/CCQDs@ZIF-8 (1)
ce:section.ce:para: MOFs with excellent luminescent properties have been applied by researchers in the field of anti-counterfeiting and information sensing [65]. Although there are many applications of fluorescent ink, there are still defects such as long reaction time and complex preparation process. In this work, 1 can be applied as a fluorescent ink to test the photo-luminescence performance under simple conditions. 1 has obvious luminescence phenomenon under the irradiation of 365 nm ultraviolet lamp, oxidized MPD and OPD can enhance its luminescence while oxidized PPD can decrease its luminescence under 365 nm ultraviolet lamp in Fig. 12 a. After the addition of oxidized OPD, the color of 1 changes to distinct yellow fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet lamp. This remarkable luminescence change makes 1 can be used an excellent anti-counterfeiting material and fluorescent ink. As a fluorescent ink, 1 has the advantages of non-toxic, easy preparation and short photo-luminescence response time.

Based on this property, in order to expand the application range of 1, 1 was prepared as a mixed matrix membrane for easy use (Fig. 12b). Agar (0.0850 g) and power of 1 (0.0024 g) were suspended in purified water (12 mL). The solution is mixed and heated to 100 ℃, the mixture is evenly and dully dissolved. The resulting suspension was cooled to room temperature, which can obtain the mixed matrix membrane. The resulting mixed matrix membrane can respond to target substances instantaneously by drop addition of PD isomers (Fig. 12c and 12d). It can also be observed that the addition of PPD can quench the fluorescence of 1 while OPD can significantly enhance the fluorescence emission of 1. Fluorescence emission of 1 was also enhanced when the newly prepared PPD was added to the surface of mixed matrix membrane. Therefore, 1 can be successfully used as fluorescent ink for anti-counterfeiting and information transfer and other fields, but also can be used as a simple test paper to detect PD isomers. Besides, as shown in Table S1, this work can compare with previous reported PD detection methods, which also reveal highly efficient recognition of PD isomers and their oxidized product [64,66–68].

In conclusion, a hybrid material fluorescein/CCQDs@ZIF-8 (1) has been successfully synthesized through in-situ encapsulation of CCQDs and fluorescein into ZIF-8 by “one-pot” method. The hybrid compound has high selectivity and accuracy to recognize PPD in these PD isomers with high sensitivity and selectivity (ratiomeric fluorescent probe with KBH: 1.85 × 103 M−1 and detection limit: 8.51 μM). Further, 1 also effectively distinguishes the oxidized product of these PD isomers. 1 can be used as a “turn-off” fluorescent probe to detect oxidized product of PPD (ratiomeric fluorescent probe with KSV: 6.82 × 102 M−1 and detection limit: 0.112 mM) and a “turn-on” fluorescent probe to detect oxidized product of MPD (ratiomeric fluorescent probe: KBH: 1.65 × 103 M−1 and detection limit: 35.03 μM in ethanol) and oxidized product of OPD (ratiomeric fluorescent probe: KBH: 2.40 × 106 M−1 and detection limit: 0.105 μM in ethanol). Hence, the hybrid material not only can specifically recognize newly prepared PPD, but also can carry out highly selective distinction and quantitative detection of the oxidized PD isomer. At present, there are few studies on the detection of PD by photo-luminescent method. This work provides the possibility for simple detection of PD isomers and its oxidized products, especially for PPD. This conclusion can be used to detect the oxidation degree of PD isomers, and timely separation of oxidation products can reduce industrial losses and improve reaction efficiency in industry. On the other hand, in daily life, PD isomers also can play the key role, such as hair coloring effect of PPD. Based on the diverse excellent fluorescence response of 1 to PPD and oxidized PD isomers, 1 can be used as a mixed matrix membrane to realize the rapid test of the target substances. This research has the wide applications to discriminate PPD and oxidized PD isomers in industrial production, environmental analysis and so on. This work also expands the application prospect of MOFs and provides a possibility for the quantitative detection of amines.
